title.zh: 卷二十七 宣張二王杜郭吳承鄭趙列傳
title.en: Volume 27: Biographies of Xuan, Zang, two Wangs, Du, Guo, Wu, Cheng, Zheng, Zhao
title.raw: 卷二十七 宣張二王杜郭吳承鄭趙列傳 Volume 27: Biographies of Xuan, Zang, two Wangs, Du, Guo, Wu, Cheng, Zheng, Zhao

sentences.zh: 宣秉字巨公，馮翊雲陽人也。

translations.literal: Xuan Bing, courtesy name Jugong, was a man of Yunyang in Fengyi commandery.
translations.idiomatic: Xuan Bing, styled Jugong, came from Yunyang in Fengyi.

sentences.zh: 少修高節，顯名三輔。

translations.literal: In youth he cultivated lofty integrity and became famous in the Three Adjuncts.
translations.idiomatic: From his youth he upheld a stern moral standard and made a name for himself across the Three Adjuncts.

sentences.zh: 哀、平際，見王氏據權專政，侵削宗室，有逆亂萌，遂隱遁深山，州郡連召，常稱疾不仕。

translations.literal: Between the reigns of Ai and Ping, seeing the Wang clan seize power and monopolize government, encroach on and diminish the imperial house, and the sprouts of rebellion and disorder appear, he thereupon hid himself in deep mountains; the province and commanderies summoned him in succession, but he constantly claimed illness and did not take office.
translations.idiomatic: Under Emperors Ai and Ping, when he saw the Wang family monopolize power, chip away at the imperial clan, and the makings of revolt take shape, he withdrew to the deep hills; though province after province called him to serve, he always pleaded illness and stayed out of office.

sentences.zh: 王莽為宰衡，辟命不應。

translations.literal: When Wang Mang was Grand Regent, he summoned him with a commission, but he did not respond.
translations.idiomatic: When Wang Mang held the title of supreme minister and summoned him to office, he refused the appointment.

sentences.zh: 及莽篡位，又遣使者征之，秉固稱疾病。

translations.literal: When Mang usurped the throne, he again sent envoys to summon him, but Bing firmly claimed illness.
translations.idiomatic: After Wang Mang seized the throne and again sent envoys for him, Xuan Bing insisted he was too ill to come.

sentences.zh: 更始即位，征為侍中。

translations.literal: When Gengshi ascended the throne, he was summoned to be Palace Attendant.
translations.idiomatic: When the Gengshi Emperor took the throne, Xuan Bing was called to court as a palace attendant.

sentences.zh: 建武元年，拜禦史中丞。

translations.literal: In the first year of Jianwu, he was appointed Imperial Clerk Grandee.
translations.idiomatic: In Jianwu 1 he was named imperial clerk grandee.

sentences.zh: 光武特詔禦史中丞與司隸校尉、尚書令會同並專席而坐，故京師號曰『三獨坐』。

translations.literal: Guangwu specially issued an edict that the Imperial Clerk Grandee, the Metropolitan Commandant, and the Director of the Secretariat should meet and sit each on a separate mat; therefore in the capital they were called the 'Three Solitary Seats.'
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Guangwu issued a special order that the imperial clerk grandee, the metropolitan commandant, and the director of the secretariat should hold joint sessions, each with his own seat—hence the capital nickname 'the three solitary seats.'

sentences.zh: 明年，遷司隸校尉。

translations.literal: The next year, he was transferred to Metropolitan Commandant.
translations.idiomatic: The following year he was promoted to metropolitan commandant.

sentences.zh: 務舉大綱，簡略苛細，百僚敬之。

translations.literal: He strove to lift up the great principles and was lenient toward harsh minutiae; the hundred officials revered him.
translations.idiomatic: He focused on the broad principles of government and eased petty severity, and the whole bureaucracy stood in awe of him.

sentences.zh: 秉性節約，常服布被，蔬食瓦器。

translations.literal: Bing by nature was frugal; he constantly wore a cloth quilt, ate plain food, and used earthenware vessels.
translations.idiomatic: By temperament he was austere: he slept under a cotton quilt, ate simple fare, and used plain pottery at table.

sentences.zh: 帝嘗幸其府舍，見而嘆曰：『楚國二龔，不如雲陽宣巨公。

translations.literal: The emperor once visited his residence and, seeing it, sighed, saying, 'The two Gongs of Chu are not equal to Jugong Xuan of Yunyang.'
translations.idiomatic: The emperor once called at his home, looked about, and exclaimed, 'The two Gongs of Chu cannot match Jugong Xuan of Yunyang.'

sentences.zh: 』即賜布帛帳帷什物。

translations.literal: Thereupon he bestowed cloth, silk, curtains, hangings, and assorted goods.
translations.idiomatic: He then gave him cloth and silk, bed hangings, and other household goods.

sentences.zh: 四年，拜大司徒司直。

translations.literal: In the fourth year, he was appointed Grandee of the Grand Minister of Education for Directorships.
translations.idiomatic: In the fourth year he was appointed grandee of the grand minister of education for directorships.

sentences.zh: 所得祿奉，輒以收養親族。

translations.literal: Whatever salary he received, he always used it to support and raise his kinsmen.
translations.idiomatic: He turned every stipend he earned toward supporting his extended family.

sentences.zh: 其孤弱者，分與田地，自無擔石之儲。

translations.literal: To the orphaned and weak he allotted fields and land; he himself had no store even of a dan or shi of grain.
translations.idiomatic: The orphaned and helpless he supplied with land, while he himself kept not even a peck of grain in reserve.

sentences.zh: 六年，卒於官，帝敏惜之，除子彪為郎。

translations.literal: In the sixth year, he died in office; the emperor grieved for him with keen regret and appointed his son Biao Gentleman.
translations.idiomatic: He died in office in the sixth year; the emperor mourned him deeply and appointed his son Xuan Biao a gentleman cadet.

sentences.zh: 張湛字子孝，扶風平陵人也。

translations.literal: Zhang Zhan, courtesy name Zixiao, was a man of Pingling in Fufeng commandery.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Zhan, styled Zixiao, was a native of Pingling in Fufeng.

sentences.zh: 矜嚴好禮，動止有則，居處幽室，必自修整，雖遇妻子，若嚴君焉。

translations.literal: He was stern and fond of ritual; in movement and rest he had rules; in dwelling in a secluded chamber he always put himself in order; even when facing wife and children, he was like a stern lord.
translations.idiomatic: Grave in manner and devoted to ceremony, he regulated every gesture; even in his private rooms he kept himself in perfect order, and before his wife and children he behaved like a magistrate before his subordinates.

sentences.zh: 及在鄉黨，詳言正色，三輔以為儀表。

translations.literal: When among the village and community, he spoke in detail with a straight countenance; the Three Adjuncts took him as a model.
translations.idiomatic: Among neighbors and townsfolk he chose his words carefully and kept a straight face, and the Three Adjuncts looked to him as their standard.

sentences.zh: 人或謂湛偽詐，湛聞而笑曰：『我誠詐也。

translations.literal: Some people said Zhan was false and deceitful; Zhan heard it and laughed, saying, 'I am indeed deceitful.'
translations.idiomatic: Some called his manner a pose; when Zhang Zhan heard this he laughed and said, 'They are right—I am putting on an act.'

sentences.zh: 人皆詐惡，我獨詐善，不亦可乎？』

translations.literal: All others feign wickedness; I alone feign goodness—is that not acceptable?'
translations.idiomatic: Everyone else pretends to be wicked; I alone pretend to be good—what is wrong with that?'

sentences.zh: 成、哀間，為二千石。

translations.literal: Between Cheng and Ai, he held office at two thousand piculs rank.
translations.idiomatic: Under Emperors Cheng and Ai he served in posts of two thousand piculs.

sentences.zh: 王莽時，歷太守、都尉。

translations.literal: In the time of Wang Mang, he successively served as grand administrator and chief commandant.
translations.idiomatic: During Wang Mang's reign he successively held the posts of grand administrator and chief commandant.

sentences.zh: 建武初，為左馮翊，在郡修典禮，設條教，政化大行。

translations.literal: At the beginning of Jianwu, he was left Administrator of Fengyi; in the commandery he restored canonical rites, set down regulations and instruction, and administrative transformation was greatly carried out.
translations.idiomatic: Early in Jianwu he became left administrator of Fengyi, where he revived the classical rituals, issued clear regulations, and saw a thorough moral transformation of the region.

sentences.zh: 後告歸平陵，望寺門而步。

translations.literal: Later he requested leave and returned to Pingling; facing the gate of the official residence he walked on foot.
translations.idiomatic: Later he took leave and returned to Pingling; when he reached the yamen gate he dismounted and went in on foot.

sentences.zh: 主簿進曰：『明府位尊德重，不宜自輕。

translations.literal: The chief clerk advanced and said, 'My lord's position is exalted and your virtue weighty; it is not fitting to slight yourself.'
translations.idiomatic: His chief clerk urged him: 'Your honor's rank and virtue are too high for you to demean yourself so.'

sentences.zh: 』湛曰：『《禮》，下公門，軾輅馬。

translations.literal: Zhan said, 'In the Classic of Rites, below the duke's gate one dismounts and leans on the carriage bar.'
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Zhan replied, 'The Classic of Rites says that below a noble's gate one leaves the chariot and touches the crossbar in respect.'

sentences.zh: 孔子於鄉黨，恂恂如也。

translations.literal: Confucius in the village and community was deferential and cautious.
translations.idiomatic: Confucius among his neighbors was modest and careful in every step.

sentences.zh: 父母之國，所宜盡禮，何謂輕哉？』

translations.literal: In the land of one's father and mother, one ought to exhaust ritual; what is called slighting?'
translations.idiomatic: In one's native place one should observe every courtesy—how is that belittling myself?'

sentences.zh: 五年，拜光祿勛。

translations.literal: In the fifth year, he was appointed Bearer of the Brightness.
translations.idiomatic: In the fifth year he was appointed bearer of the brightness, superintendent of the imperial household.

sentences.zh: 光武臨朝，或有惰容，湛輒陳諫其失。

translations.literal: When Guangwu held court, sometimes there was a slack expression; Zhan always remonstrated by stating his faults.
translations.idiomatic: Whenever Emperor Guangwu looked slack at audience, Zhang Zhan would speak up and correct him.

sentences.zh: 常乘白馬，帝每見湛，輒言『白馬生且復諫矣』。

translations.literal: He constantly rode a white horse; whenever the emperor saw Zhan, he would say, 'The white-horse gentleman is about to remonstrate again.'
translations.idiomatic: He habitually rode a white horse, so whenever the emperor saw him coming he would mutter, 'Here comes the white horse—ready to lecture me again.'

sentences.zh: 七年，以病乞身，拜光祿大夫，代王丹為太子太傅。

translations.literal: In the seventh year, citing illness he begged to retire; he was appointed Grandee of the Imperial Household and replaced Wang Dan as tutor to the heir apparent.
translations.idiomatic: In the seventh year he asked to retire on grounds of ill health; the court named him grandee of the imperial household and tutor to the crown prince in place of Wang Dan.

sentences.zh: 及郭后廢，因稱疾不朝，拜太中大夫，居中東門候舍，故時人號曰中東門君。

translations.literal: When Empress Guo was deposed, he thereupon claimed illness and did not attend court; he was appointed Grandee of the Palace and dwelt in the hostel at the Central East Gate, so people of the time called him Lord Central East Gate.
translations.idiomatic: After Empress Guo was cast aside he stayed away from court, pleading sickness; he was made grandee of the palace and lived in the lodge at the Central East Gate, so contemporaries dubbed him 'the lord of Central East Gate.'

sentences.zh: 帝數存問賞賜。

translations.literal: The emperor repeatedly inquired after him with gifts.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor often sent messengers to ask after his health and showered him with gifts.

sentences.zh: 後大徒戴涉被誅，帝強起湛以代之。

translations.literal: Later Grand Minister of Education Dai She was executed; the emperor forced Zhan to rise and replace him.
translations.idiomatic: Later, when Grand Minister of Education Dai She was put to death, the emperor insisted on dragging Zhang Zhan from retirement to fill the post.

sentences.zh: 湛至朝堂，遺失溲便，因自陳疾篤，不能復任朝事，遂罷之。

translations.literal: When Zhan reached the court hall, he lost control of his bladder; thereupon he himself stated his illness was grave and he could not again take up court affairs, and he was thereupon dismissed.
translations.idiomatic: At the hall of state he suffered a public loss of bladder control; he then declared himself too ill for any further duty and was allowed to step down.

sentences.zh: 後數年，卒於家。

translations.literal: Several years later, he died at home.
translations.idiomatic: A few years later he died at home.

sentences.zh: 王丹字仲回，京兆下邽人也。

translations.literal: Wang Dan, courtesy name Zhonghui, was a man of Xiaqi in the metropolitan region of Jingzhao.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Dan, styled Zhonghui, came from Xiaqi in the Jingzhao area around the capital.

sentences.zh: 哀、平時，仕州郡。

translations.literal: In the time of Ai and Ping, he served in province and commandery.
translations.idiomatic: Under Emperors Ai and Ping he held office in provincial and commandery administration.

sentences.zh: 王莽時，連征不至。

translations.literal: In the time of Wang Mang, he was repeatedly summoned but did not arrive.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Mang summoned him repeatedly, but he never answered the call.

sentences.zh: 家累千金，隱居養誌，好施周急。

translations.literal: His family accumulated wealth to a thousand in gold; he lived in reclusion cultivating his purpose, was fond of giving and relieving the urgent.
translations.idiomatic: Though his household was worth a thousand pounds of gold, he lived in retirement, nurturing his aims, generous to those in sudden need.

sentences.zh: 每歲農時，輒載酒肴於田間，候勤者而勞之。

translations.literal: Every year at farming season, he would load wine and food in the fields and wait for the diligent and reward them.
translations.idiomatic: Each spring he would drive wine and meat to the fields and toast the hardest-working farmers.

sentences.zh: 其墮懶者，恥不致丹，皆兼功自厲。

translations.literal: Those who were lazy were ashamed not to reach Dan's standard, and all doubled their effort and urged themselves on.
translations.idiomatic: The idle felt ashamed to fall short of his example and doubled their efforts.

sentences.zh: 邑聚相率，以致殷富。

translations.literal: Villages and hamlets led one another and thereby became abundant and rich.
translations.idiomatic: Hamlet after hamlet took his lead and grew prosperous.

sentences.zh: 其輕黠遊蕩廢業為患者，輒曉其父兄，使黜責之。

translations.literal: As for those who were frivolous, cunning, roamed and wandered, abandoned their work and became a harm, he always enlightened their fathers and brothers and made them dismiss and reprove them.
translations.idiomatic: Young men who loafed, schemed, and made trouble he would take aside with their fathers and elder brothers until they were chastened back to work.

sentences.zh: 沒者則賻給，親自將護。

translations.literal: Those who died, he assisted with funeral gifts and personally oversaw the arrangements.
translations.idiomatic: For the dead he paid burial costs and saw to the rites himself.

sentences.zh: 其有遭喪憂者，輒待丹為辦，鄉鄰以為常。

translations.literal: Those who suffered bereavement and grief, he always waited for Dan to handle it; the village neighbors took it as normal.
translations.idiomatic: Families in mourning learned to wait for Wang Dan to arrange everything; neighbors came to expect it.

sentences.zh: 行之十餘年，其化大洽，風俗以篤。

translations.literal: He practiced this for more than ten years; his transformation was greatly harmonious and customs thereby became earnest.
translations.idiomatic: He kept this up for over a decade until custom ran deep and the whole countryside grew earnest and kind.

sentences.zh: 丹資性方潔，疾惡強豪。

translations.literal: Dan by nature was upright and clean, hated the evil and the powerful and grand.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Dan was square and scrupulous by nature, and detested bullies and grandees.

sentences.zh: 時，河南太守同郡陳遵，關西之大俠也。

translations.literal: At that time the grand administrator of Henan, Chen Zun of the same commandery, was a great knight-errant west of the pass.
translations.idiomatic: At the time Chen Zun, grand administrator of Henan and a fellow townsman, was famed as the greatest bravo west of the passes.

sentences.zh: 其友人喪親，遵為護喪事，賻助甚豐。

translations.literal: When a friend of his lost a parent, Zun undertook the funeral affairs for him, and the condolence gifts were very abundant.
translations.idiomatic: When a friend's parent died, Chen Zun staged the funeral and poured in lavish contributions.

sentences.zh: 丹乃懷縑一匹，陳之於主人前，曰：『如丹此縑，出自機杼。

translations.literal: Dan then carried in his bosom one bolt of silk, displayed it before the host, and said, 'Such silk as Dan has here comes from the loom.'
translations.idiomatic: Wang Dan brought only a single bolt of silk, laid it before the bereaved family, and said, 'This cloth is thread I wove myself.'

sentences.zh: 』遵聞而有慚色。

translations.literal: When Zun heard, he had a look of shame.
translations.idiomatic: Chen Zun flushed with shame when he heard that.

sentences.zh: 自以知名，欲結交於丹，丹拒而不許。

translations.literal: Considering himself famous, he wished to befriend Dan, but Dan refused and did not permit it.
translations.idiomatic: Confident in his own reputation, Chen Zun sought Wang Dan's friendship; Wang Dan turned him down flat.

sentences.zh: 會前將軍鄧禹西征關中，軍糧乏，丹率宗族上表二千斛。

translations.literal: When former general Deng Yu campaigned west into Guanzhong, army provisions were lacking; Dan led his clan to present a memorial offering two thousand hu of grain.
translations.idiomatic: When General Deng Yu marched into Guanzhong and supplies ran short, Wang Dan led his kinsmen in offering two thousand hu of grain to the throne.

sentences.zh: 禹在領左馮翊，稱疾不視事，免歸。

translations.literal: Yu was concurrently left Administrator of Fengyi; he claimed illness and did not attend to affairs, and was dismissed and returned home.
translations.idiomatic: Deng Yu was also left administrator of Fengyi; he pleaded illness, neglected the yamen, and was removed to go home.

sentences.zh: 後征為太子少傅。

translations.literal: Later he was summoned to be Junior Tutor to the heir apparent.
translations.idiomatic: Later Wang Dan was recalled as junior tutor to the crown prince.

sentences.zh: 時，大司徒侯霸欲與交友，及丹被征，遣子昱候於道。

translations.literal: At that time Grand Minister of Education Hou Ba wished to befriend him, and when Dan was summoned, he sent his son Yu to wait on the road.
translations.idiomatic: Grand Minister Hou Ba wanted his friendship; when Wang Dan was summoned to court, Hou sent his son Hou Yu to greet him on the road.

sentences.zh: 昱迎拜車下，丹下答之。

translations.literal: Yu greeted and bowed below the carriage; Dan descended and returned the bow.
translations.idiomatic: Hou Yu bowed at the carriage step; Wang Dan stepped down and returned the courtesy.

sentences.zh: 昱曰：『家公欲與君結交，何為見拜？

translations.literal: Yu said, 'My father wishes to befriend you; why do you return the bow?'
translations.idiomatic: Hou Yu said, 'My father means to befriend you—why do you bow back as if to an equal?'

sentences.zh: 』丹曰：『君房有是言，丹未之許也。』

translations.literal: Dan said, 'If Junfang had such words, Dan has not assented to them.'
translations.idiomatic: Wang Dan replied, 'If your father said that, I have never agreed to it.'

sentences.zh: 丹子有同門生喪家，家在中山，白丹欲往奔慰。

translations.literal: Dan's son had a fellow student whose family had a bereavement; the home was in Zhongshan, and he informed Dan wishing to go hasten to console.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Dan's son had a classmate whose family was in mourning in Zhongshan; the boy asked leave to rush there in person.

sentences.zh: 結侶將行，丹怒而撻之，令寄縑以祠焉。

translations.literal: He tied companions and was about to set out; Dan angrily flogged him and ordered him to send silk by post for sacrifice there.
translations.idiomatic: He was gathering travel companions when Wang Dan beat him in anger and told him to send a bolt of silk for the rites instead.

sentences.zh: 或問其故，丹曰：『交道之難，未易言也。

translations.literal: Someone asked the reason; Dan said, 'The difficulty of friendship is not easy to speak of.'
translations.idiomatic: Asked why, Wang Dan said, 'The pitfalls of friendship are not easily put into words.'

sentences.zh: 世稱管、鮑，次則王、貢。

translations.literal: The age praises Guan and Bao; next come Wang and Gong.
translations.idiomatic: The world praises Guan Zhong and Bao Shuya; next come Wang Ji and Gong Yu.

sentences.zh: 張、陳兇其終，蕭，朱隙其末，故知全之者鮮矣。

translations.literal: Zhang and Chen met violent ends; Xiao and Zhu fell out at the last—therefore one knows those who preserve friendship to the end are few.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Er and Chen Yu destroyed each other; Xiao Wangzhi and Zhu Bo fell out at the end—so few friendships stay whole.

sentences.zh: 』時人服其言。

translations.literal: People of the time submitted to his words.
translations.idiomatic: His contemporaries conceded the point.

sentences.zh: 客初有薦士於丹者，因選舉之，而後所舉者陷罪，丹坐以免。

translations.literal: A client at first had recommended a scholar to Dan; thereupon he selected and raised him, but afterward the one he had raised was implicated in a crime, and Dan sat for it and was dismissed.
translations.idiomatic: A retainer once recommended a man whom Wang Dan then advanced; when that man was later convicted, Wang Dan lost his post on account of it.

sentences.zh: 客慚懼自絕，而丹終無所言。

translations.literal: The client was ashamed and afraid and broke off relations of himself, but Dan to the end said nothing.
translations.idiomatic: The retainer, mortified, cut off all contact, yet Wang Dan never breathed a word of reproach.

sentences.zh: 尋復征為太子太傅，乃呼客謂曰：『子之自絕，何量丹之薄也？

translations.literal: Soon he was again summoned as tutor to the heir apparent; he then called the client and said, 'Your breaking off—how you underestimated Dan's meanness?'
translations.idiomatic: Soon recalled as tutor to the crown prince, he summoned the same retainer and said, 'You dropped me as if I were too small-minded to understand?'

sentences.zh: 』不為設食以罰之，相待如舊。

translations.literal: He did not set out food for him as a punishment, but treated him as before.
translations.idiomatic: He withheld a meal as a light rebuke, then treated him exactly as before.

sentences.zh: 其後遜位，卒於家。

translations.literal: Afterward he yielded his position and died at home.
translations.idiomatic: He later resigned his post and died at home.

sentences.zh: 王良字仲子，東海蘭陵人也。

translations.literal: Wang Liang, courtesy name Zhongzi, was a man of Lanling in Donghai commandery.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Liang, styled Zhongzi, was from Lanling in Donghai.

sentences.zh: 少好學，習《小夏侯尚書》。

translations.literal: In youth he was fond of learning and studied the Lesser Xiahou Documents.
translations.idiomatic: In youth he loved learning and mastered the Lesser Xiahou version of the Documents.

sentences.zh: 王莽時，寢病不仕，教授諸生千餘人。

translations.literal: In the time of Wang Mang, he lay ill and did not take office, and taught more than a thousand students.
translations.idiomatic: Under Wang Mang he stayed in bed and refused office, while instructing over a thousand pupils.

sentences.zh: 建武二年，大司馬吳漢辟，不應。

translations.literal: In the second year of Jianwu, Grand Marshal Wu Han summoned him; he did not respond.
translations.idiomatic: In Jianwu 2 Grand Marshal Wu Han offered him a post; he declined.

sentences.zh: 三年，征拜諫議太夫，數有忠言，以禮進止，朝廷敬之。

translations.literal: In the third year, he was summoned and appointed Grandee Remonstrant; he repeatedly had loyal words, advanced and withdrew according to ritual, and the court revered him.
translations.idiomatic: In the third year he was called to be grandee remonstrant; he spoke frankly, observed every ritual nicety in court, and the bureaucracy respected him.

sentences.zh: 遷沛郡太守。

translations.literal: He was transferred to grand administrator of Pei commandery.
translations.idiomatic: He was then moved to grand administrator of Pei.

sentences.zh: 至蘄縣，稱病不之府，官屬皆隨就之，良遂上疾篤，乞骸骨，征拜太中大夫。

translations.literal: When he reached Qi county, he claimed illness and did not go to the official residence; the subordinate officials all followed and went to him; Liang thereupon submitted that his illness was grave, begged for his bones, and was summoned and appointed Grandee of the Palace.
translations.idiomatic: At Qi he pleaded illness and never entered the yamen; his staff trooped out to him instead. He then memorialized that he was dying, asked to retire, and was made grandee of the palace.

sentences.zh: 六年，代宣秉為大司徒司直。

translations.literal: In the sixth year, he replaced Xuan Bing as Grandee of the Grand Minister of Education for Directorships.
translations.idiomatic: In the sixth year he succeeded Xuan Bing as grandee of the grand minister of education for directorships.

sentences.zh: 在位恭儉，妻子不入官舍，布被瓦器。

translations.literal: In position he was respectful and frugal; wife and children did not enter the official residence; he had a cloth quilt and earthenware vessels.
translations.idiomatic: In office he was humble and spare: his wife and children never set foot in the yamen, and his quarters held only a cotton quilt and pottery vessels.

sentences.zh: 時，司徒史鮑恢以事到東海，過候其家，而良妻布裙曳柴，從田中歸。

translations.literal: At that time a clerk of the Minister of Education, Bao Hui, on business came to Donghai, passed to inquire at his home, and Liang's wife in cloth skirt was dragging firewood and returned from the fields.
translations.idiomatic: Once a ministerial clerk named Bao Hui came to Donghai on business and called at Wang Liang's house; Wang Liang's wife was dragging firewood home from the fields in a homespun skirt.

sentences.zh: 恢告曰：『我司徒史也，故來受書，欲見夫人。

translations.literal: Hui announced, saying, 'I am a clerk of the Minister of Education; I have come to receive documents and wish to see the lady.'
translations.idiomatic: Bao Hui announced himself: 'I am a clerk of the minister of education, here to collect papers—I should like to see your mistress.'

sentences.zh: 』妻曰：『妾是也。

translations.literal: The wife said, 'Your handmaid is she.'
translations.idiomatic: She replied, 'I am the woman of the house.'

sentences.zh: 若掾，無書。

translations.literal: Sir clerk, there are no documents.'
translations.idiomatic: But, sir, there are no papers here.'

sentences.zh: 』恢乃下拜，嘆息而還，聞者莫不嘉之。

translations.literal: Hui thereupon descended and bowed, sighed and returned; those who heard were without exception praising it.
translations.idiomatic: Bao Hui bowed low, sighed, and withdrew; everyone who heard the story praised her.

sentences.zh: 後以病歸，一歲復征，至滎陽，疾篤不任進道，乃過其友人。

translations.literal: Later citing illness he returned home; after one year he was again summoned; when he reached Xingyang, his illness was grave and he could not undertake to advance on the road, and he passed by his friend.
translations.idiomatic: Later he went home on sick leave; a year later he was summoned again, but at Xingyang he was too ill to travel on and stopped with a friend.

sentences.zh: 友人不肯見，曰：『不有忠言奇謀而取大位，何其往來屑屑不憚煩也？

translations.literal: The friend was unwilling to see him and said, 'Without loyal words or strange stratagems you took a great position—how is it that you come and go fussily and do not tire of the trouble?'
translations.idiomatic: The friend refused to receive him, saying, 'You reached high rank without a single memorable counsel—why do you keep shuttling back and forth so busily?'

sentences.zh: 』遂拒之。

translations.literal: Thereupon he rejected him.
translations.idiomatic: He turned Wang Liang away.

sentences.zh: 良慚，自後連征，輒稱病。

translations.literal: Liang was ashamed; from then on whenever repeatedly summoned, he always claimed illness.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Liang was mortified; from then on every new summons met the excuse of illness.

sentences.zh: 詔以玄纁聘之，遂不應。

translations.literal: An edict used dark silk to hire him; he thereupon did not respond.
translations.idiomatic: The court sent the formal black-and-red gift bundle to engage him; he still would not answer.

sentences.zh: 後光武幸蘭陵，遣使者問良所苦疾，不能言對。

translations.literal: Later Guangwu visited Lanling and sent an envoy to ask what illness Liang suffered; he could not speak in reply.
translations.idiomatic: Later, when Emperor Guangwu toured Lanling, he sent to ask what ailed Wang Liang; Wang Liang could not bring himself to reply.

sentences.zh: 詔復其子孫邑中徭役，卒於家。

translations.literal: An edict remitted corvée labor for his descendants in the village; he died at home.
translations.idiomatic: An edict exempted his descendants from local corvée; he died at home.

sentences.zh: 論曰：夫利仁者或借仁以從利，體義者不期體以合義。

translations.literal: The disquisition says: Those who profit by benevolence perhaps borrow benevolence to follow profit; those who embody rightness do not expect embodiment in order to match rightness.
translations.idiomatic: The historian remarks: Some who speak of humanity only borrow it for gain; those who truly live by duty do not perform duty for show.

sentences.zh: 季文子妾不衣帛，魯人以為美談。

translations.literal: Ji Wenzi's concubines did not wear silk; the people of Lu took it as a fine tale.
translations.idiomatic: Ji Wenzi's concubines wore no silk; the people of Lu praised it as a noble example.

sentences.zh: 公孫弘身服布被，汲黯譏其多詐。

translations.literal: Gongsun Hong personally wore a cloth quilt; Ji An ridiculed him as greatly deceitful.
translations.idiomatic: Gongsun Hong affected a cotton quilt while drawing a minister's salary; Ji An called him out for shameless pretense.

sentences.zh: 事實未殊而譽毀別議。

translations.literal: The facts were not different yet praise and blame were separately debated.
translations.idiomatic: The facts were much the same, yet one man was lauded and the other mocked.

sentences.zh: 何也？

translations.literal: Why?
translations.idiomatic: Why was that?

sentences.zh: 將體之與利之異乎？

translations.literal: Is embodying it different from profiting by it?
translations.idiomatic: Is there a difference between living a principle and merely profiting from its reputation?

sentences.zh: 宣秉、王良處位優重，而秉甘疏薄，良妻荷薪，可謂行過乎儉。

translations.literal: Xuan Bing and Wang Liang occupied positions that were honored and weighty, yet Bing was willing to be sparse and thin, and Liang's wife carried firewood—one may say their conduct exceeded in frugality.
translations.idiomatic: Xuan Bing and Wang Liang held exalted posts, yet Xuan Bing embraced the barest living and Wang Liang's wife hauled firewood home—both carried austerity past what the world expected.

sentences.zh: 然當世咨其清，人君高其節，豈非臨之以誠哉！

translations.literal: Yet the age consulted on their purity, and rulers of men esteemed their integrity—was it not because they were faced with sincerity!
translations.idiomatic: Still their contemporaries praised their purity, and their sovereigns honored their integrity—surely because genuine virtue commands respect!

sentences.zh: 語曰：『同言而信，則信在言前；

translations.literal: A saying states, 'When the same words are trusted, then trust lies before the words;
translations.idiomatic: The proverb runs: when people already trust you, your word arrives with credit to spare;

sentences.zh: 同令而行，則誠在令外。

translations.literal: when the same orders are carried out, then sincerity lies beyond the orders.'
translations.idiomatic: when they obey your commands without hesitation, your good faith has already passed beyond the written order.'

sentences.zh: 』不其然乎！

translations.literal: 'Is it not so!'
translations.idiomatic: Is that not exactly how it works!'

sentences.zh: 張湛不屑矜偽之誚，斯不偽矣。

translations.literal: Zhang Zhan disdained to mind the ridicule of affected pretense—thus he was not pretentious.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Zhan brushed off charges of affectation, and in doing so proved he was not posing.

sentences.zh: 王丹難於交執之道，斯知交矣。

translations.literal: Wang Dan found difficult the way of holding fast in friendship—thus he knew friendship.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Dan treated friendship as a grave trust—so he truly understood what friendship costs.

sentences.zh: 杜林字伯山，扶風茂陵人也。

translations.literal: Du Lin, courtesy name Boshan, was a man of Maoling in Fufeng commandery.
translations.idiomatic: Du Lin, styled Boshan, came from Maoling in Fufeng.

sentences.zh: 父鄴，成、哀間為涼州刺史。

translations.literal: His father Ye, between Cheng and Ai, was Inspector of Liang province.
translations.idiomatic: His father Du Ye served as inspector of Liang province under Emperors Cheng and Ai.

sentences.zh: 林少好學沈深，家既多書，又外氏張竦父子喜文采，林從竦受學，博洽多聞，時稱通儒。

translations.literal: Lin in youth was fond of learning and profound; the household already had many books, and moreover his mother's kin Zhang Song and his son were fond of literary polish; Lin received instruction from Song, was broadly learned and heard much, and the age called him a thorough Confucian.
translations.idiomatic: Du Lin loved learning from boyhood and had a reflective cast of mind; his family owned a large library, and his maternal relatives Zhang Song and son delighted in belles lettres—Du Lin studied under Zhang Song, became encyclopedic in learning, and his generation hailed him as a consummate scholar.

sentences.zh: 初為郡吏。

translations.literal: At first he was a clerk of the commandery.
translations.idiomatic: He began as a commandery clerk.

sentences.zh: 王莽敗，盜賊起，林與弟成及同郡範逡、孟冀等，將細弱俱客河西。

translations.literal: When Wang Mang fell, bandits arose; Lin together with his younger brother Cheng and Fan Sui, Meng Ji, and others of the same commandery led the women and children and together sojourned west of the River.
translations.idiomatic: When Wang Mang fell and banditry flared, Du Lin fled west of the Yellow River with his brother Du Cheng, Fan Sui, Meng Ji, and others from the commandery, taking women and children along.

sentences.zh: 道逢賊數千人，遂掠取財裝，褫奪衣服，拔刃向林等將欲殺之。

translations.literal: On the road they encountered several thousand bandits, who thereupon plundered their goods and baggage, stripped their clothing, drew blades toward Lin and the others and were about to kill them.
translations.idiomatic: They met several thousand brigands who robbed them bare, stripped their clothes, and leveled swords as if to slaughter them all.

sentences.zh: 冀仰曰：『願一言而死。

translations.literal: Ji looked up and said, 'I wish one word and then to die.'
translations.idiomatic: Meng Ji looked up and said, 'Let me speak one sentence before I die.'

sentences.zh: 將軍知天神乎？

translations.literal: General, do you understand the spirits of Heaven?
translations.idiomatic: General, do you know how Heaven judges men?

sentences.zh: 赤眉兵眾百萬，所向無前，而殘賊不道，卒至破敗。

translations.literal: The Red Eyebrows had troops in the millions, wherever they went none stood before them, yet they were cruel bandits without the Way, and in the end arrived at defeat and destruction.
translations.idiomatic: The Red Eyebrows mustered a million men and swept all before them, yet their cruelty and lawlessness brought them to ruin in the end.

sentences.zh: 今將軍以數千之眾，欲規霸王之事，不行仁恩而反遵覆車，不畏天乎？

translations.literal: Now the general, with a host of only several thousand, wishes to plan the affairs of a hegemon-king; you do not practice benevolence and favor but on the contrary follow the overturned cart—do you not fear Heaven?
translations.idiomatic: You command only a few thousand yet dream of hegemony; instead of kindness you repeat the mistake of those who fell before—do you not fear Heaven's anger?

sentences.zh: 』賊遂釋之，俱免於難。

translations.literal: The bandits thereupon released them, and all were spared from disaster.
translations.idiomatic: The bandits let them go, and the whole party walked free.

sentences.zh: 隗囂素聞林誌節，深相敬待，以為持書平。

translations.literal: Wei Ao had long heard of Lin's purpose and integrity, treated him with deep respect, and appointed him examining clerk for palace documents.
translations.idiomatic: Wei Ao, who had long admired Du Lin's integrity, received him with great respect and named him examining clerk for palace documents.

sentences.zh: 後因疾告去，辭還祿食。

translations.literal: Later citing illness he requested to depart, declined and returned salary and rations.
translations.idiomatic: Later he asked leave on grounds of illness, resigned his stipend, and returned his grain allowance.

sentences.zh: 囂復欲令強起，遂稱篤。

translations.literal: Ao again wished to make him forcibly rise, and he thereupon claimed grave illness.
translations.idiomatic: When Wei Ao tried to force him back to office, Du Lin pleaded a mortal illness.

sentences.zh: 囂意雖相望，且欲優容之，乃出令曰：『杜伯山天子所不能臣，諸侯所不能友，蓋伯夷、叔齊恥食周粟。

translations.literal: Ao's mind though still looked to him, and moreover wished to treat him with generous tolerance, thereupon issued an order saying, 'Du Boshan is one whom the Son of Heaven could not make his subject, whom the feudal lords could not make his friend—he is like Boyi and Shuqi, who were ashamed to eat Zhou grain.
translations.idiomatic: Wei Ao still wanted his service yet meant to indulge him, and issued a proclamation: 'Du Boshan cannot be bound as a subject to the Son of Heaven nor kept as a friend by any prince—he is another Boyi and Shuqi, ashamed to eat the grain of the usurping Zhou.'

sentences.zh: 今且從師友之位，須道開通，使順所誌。

translations.literal: For now let him occupy the place of teacher-friend; wait until the Way is open and unobstructed, and cause him to follow what he purposes.'
translations.idiomatic: For the present treat him as mentor and companion; when the road is clear again, let him follow his own resolve.'

sentences.zh: 』林雖拘於囂，而終不屈節。

translations.literal: Although Lin was detained by Ao, to the end he did not bend his integrity.
translations.idiomatic: Though Wei Ao held him fast, Du Lin never compromised his principles.

sentences.zh: 建武六年，弟成物故，囂乃聽林持喪東歸。

translations.literal: In the sixth year of Jianwu, his younger brother Cheng died; Ao then permitted Lin to hold mourning and return east.
translations.idiomatic: In Jianwu 6 his brother Du Cheng died; Wei Ao finally allowed Du Lin to escort the body home to the east.

sentences.zh: 既遣而悔，追令刺客楊賢於隴坻遮殺之。

translations.literal: After dispatching him he regretted it, and pursued with orders for the assassin Yang Xian to intercept and kill him at Longdi.
translations.idiomatic: He regretted the decision as soon as Du Lin left and sent the assassin Yang Xian to waylay and murder him at Long Slope.

sentences.zh: 賢見林身推鹿車，載致弟喪，乃嘆曰：『當今之世，誰能行義？

translations.literal: Xian saw Lin personally pushing a deer cart, bearing his younger brother's funeral, and sighed, saying, 'In the present age, who can practice righteousness?
translations.idiomatic: Yang Xian found Du Lin alone, pushing a handcart loaded with his brother's coffin, and sighed, 'In times like these, who still keeps faith with duty?

sentences.zh: 我雖小人，何忍殺義士！

translations.literal: I, though a petty man, how could I bear to kill a man of righteousness!'
translations.idiomatic: I am a common fellow, yet how could I strike down a man of honor!'

sentences.zh: 』因亡去。

translations.literal: 'Thereupon he fled away.
translations.idiomatic: With that he slipped away and left the job undone.

sentences.zh: 光武聞林已還三輔，乃征拜侍禦史，引見，問以經書故舊及西州事，甚悅之，賜車馬衣被。

translations.literal: When Guangwu heard that Lin had already returned to the Three Adjuncts, he thereupon summoned and appointed him Attendant Imperial Clerk, introduced him in audience, asked him about the classics, old affairs, and matters of the western provinces, was very pleased with him, and bestowed carriage, horses, clothing, and bedding.
translations.idiomatic: Learning that Du Lin had reached the capital region, Emperor Guangwu summoned him as attendant censor, received him in audience, questioned him on the classics, old friends, and the western frontier, took great pleasure in his answers, and showered him with carriages, horses, and fine clothes.

sentences.zh: 群寮知林以名德用，甚尊憚之。

translations.literal: The host of officials knew Lin was employed for name and virtue, and greatly revered and feared him.
translations.idiomatic: The whole bureaucracy knew he had been chosen for character as much as learning, and treated him with awe.

sentences.zh: 京師士大夫，鹹推其博洽。

translations.literal: The gentlemen grandees of the capital all praised his broad learning.
translations.idiomatic: Every scholar-official at court extolled his erudition.

sentences.zh: 河南鄭興、東海衛宏等，皆長於古學。

translations.literal: Zheng Xing of Henan, Wei Hong of Donghai, and others were all long in ancient learning.
translations.idiomatic: Zheng Xing of Henan, Wei Hong of Donghai, and their circle excelled in classical scholarship.

sentences.zh: 興嘗師事劉歆，林既遇之，欣然言曰：『林得興等固諧矣，使宏得林，且有以益之。

translations.literal: Xing had once served Liu Xin as teacher; when Lin encountered him, he happily said, 'For Lin to obtain Xing and the like is indeed harmony; if Hong could obtain Lin, there would yet be something by which to enrich him.'
translations.idiomatic: Zheng Xing had studied under Liu Xin; when Du Lin met him he exclaimed, 'To win colleagues like Zheng Xing is harmony enough for me; were Wei Hong to study with me, he would gain still more.'

sentences.zh: 』及宏見林，闇然而服。

translations.literal: When Hong saw Lin, he was silent and submitted.
translations.idiomatic: When Wei Hong met Du Lin he fell silent, utterly won over.

sentences.zh: 濟南徐巡，始師事宏，後皆更受林學。

translations.literal: Xu Xun of Jinan at first served Hong as teacher; afterward both received Lin's learning anew.
translations.idiomatic: Xu Xun of Jinan had first studied under Wei Hong; both men then sat at Du Lin's feet for further instruction.

sentences.zh: 林前於西州得漆書《古文尚書》一卷，常寶愛之，雖遭難困，握持不離身。

translations.literal: Lin earlier in the western provinces had obtained one scroll of lacquer-ink text of the Old Text Documents; he constantly treasured and loved it; although he encountered hardship and distress, he grasped and held it and did not leave his person.
translations.idiomatic: In the west Du Lin had acquired a single lacquer-ink manuscript of the Old Text Book of Documents, which he cherished through every hardship and never let leave his side.

sentences.zh: 出以示宏等曰：『林流離兵亂，常恐斯經將絕。

translations.literal: He brought it out to show Hong and the others, saying, 'Lin wandered in military chaos and always feared this classic would be cut off.'
translations.idiomatic: He showed it to Wei Hong and the rest, saying, 'I wandered through war and always feared this text would perish with me.'

sentences.zh: 何意東海衛子、濟南徐生復能傳之，是道竟不墜於地也。

translations.literal: Who would have thought that Wei Zi of Donghai and Xu Sheng of Jinan could again transmit it—thus the Way in the end did not fall to the ground.'
translations.idiomatic: Who would have thought Wei Zi of Donghai and Xu Sheng of Jinan would take it up again—so the teaching never touched the earth.'

sentences.zh: 古文雖不合時務，然願諸生無悔所學。

translations.literal: The old text though does not suit present affairs, yet I wish you gentlemen not to regret what you have studied.'
translations.idiomatic: The old-text version may not suit current politics, yet I urge you not to regret the learning you have won.'

sentences.zh: 』宏、巡益重之，於是古文遂行。

translations.literal: Hong and Xun increasingly esteemed him; thereupon the old text circulated.
translations.idiomatic: Wei Hong and Xu Xun prized him all the more, and the Old Text school spread from that moment.

sentences.zh: 明年，大議郊祀制，多以為周郊後稷，漢當祀堯。

translations.literal: The next year, in the great deliberation on suburban sacrifice regulations, most held that Zhou sacrificed to Hou Ji at the suburbs, and Han ought to sacrifice to Yao.
translations.idiomatic: The next year the court debated suburban sacrifice: most argued that since the Zhou honored Hou Ji at the outer altars, Han should honor Yao.

sentences.zh: 詔復下公卿議，議者僉同，帝亦然之。

translations.literal: An edict again sent it down to the dukes and ministers for deliberation; the deliberators were all in agreement, and the emperor also approved it.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor referred the matter again to the high ministers, who agreed unanimously, and he was inclined to follow them.

sentences.zh: 林獨以為周室之興，祚由後稷，漢業特起，功不緣堯。

translations.literal: Lin alone held that the rise of the Zhou house, its blessing came from Hou Ji; the Han enterprise arose especially, its merit did not follow from Yao.
translations.idiomatic: Du Lin alone argued that Zhou rose through the virtue of Hou Ji, whereas Han's mandate sprang from its own founders, not from Yao's line.

sentences.zh: 祖宗故事，所宜因循。

translations.literal: The precedents of forefathers and ancestors are what ought to be followed and continued.
translations.idiomatic: The ancestral precedents of the dynasty should be kept as they stand.

sentences.zh: 定從林議。

translations.literal: The decision followed Lin's proposal.
translations.idiomatic: The court settled on Du Lin's view.

sentences.zh: 後代王良為大司徒司直。

translations.literal: Later he replaced Wang Liang as Grandee of the Grand Minister of Education for Directorships.
translations.idiomatic: He later succeeded Wang Liang as grandee of the grand minister of education for directorships.

sentences.zh: 林薦同郡範逡、趙秉、申屠剛及隴西牛邯等，皆被擢用，士多歸之。

translations.literal: Lin recommended Fan Sui, Zhao Bing, Shen Tu Gang, and Niu Han of Longxi, and others of the same sort; all were promoted and employed, and many scholars attached to him.
translations.idiomatic: He recommended Fan Sui, Zhao Bing, Shen Tu Gang, Niu Han of Longxi, and others; each won appointment, and talent flocked to his banner.

sentences.zh: 十一年，司直官罷，以林代郭憲為光祿勛。

translations.literal: In the eleventh year, the office of grandee for directorships was abolished, and Lin replaced Guo Xian as Bearer of the Brightness.
translations.idiomatic: In the eleventh year the directorship post was abolished, and Du Lin replaced Guo Xian as bearer of the brightness.

sentences.zh: 內奉宿衛，外總三署，周密敬慎，選舉稱平。

translations.literal: Within he attended night guard; without he oversaw the three bureaus; he was thorough, careful, and respectful, and in selection and promotion he was called even.
translations.idiomatic: Within the palace he stood night watch; outside he superintended the three cadet bureaus; he was meticulous and even-handed in appointments.

sentences.zh: 郎有好學者，輒見誘進，朝夕滿堂。

translations.literal: When gentlemen cadets were fond of learning, he always saw them enticed forward; morning and evening the hall was full.
translations.idiomatic: Whenever a gentleman cadet showed scholarly promise, Du Lin drew him forward until his hall teemed with students morning and night.

sentences.zh: 十四年，群臣上言：『古者肉刑嚴重，則人畏法令；

translations.literal: In the fourteenth year, the host of ministers submitted words, saying, 'In antiquity corporal punishment was severe, then the people feared laws and orders;
translations.idiomatic: In the fourteenth year ministers memorialized: 'Under the ancients harsh mutilation made the people dread the law;

sentences.zh: 今憲律輕薄，故奸軌不勝。

translations.literal: now statutes and ordinances are light and thin, therefore treachery and villainy cannot be overcome.'
translations.idiomatic: today our code is so mild that crime runs beyond control.'

sentences.zh: 宜增科禁，以防其源。

translations.literal: It is fitting to increase statutes and prohibitions, in order to guard its source.'
translations.idiomatic: We should tighten the statutes at the root to choke off wrongdoing.'

sentences.zh: 』詔下公卿。

translations.literal: 'An edict sent it down to the dukes and ministers.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor referred the proposal to his ministers.

sentences.zh: 林奏曰：『夫人情挫辱，則義節之風損，法防繁多，則敬免之行興。

translations.literal: Lin memorialized, saying, 'As for human feelings, when they are humiliated and insulted, then the wind of righteousness and integrity is damaged; when legal safeguards are numerous, then conduct of mere evasion of punishment arises.
translations.idiomatic: Du Lin answered with a memorial: 'When the law humiliates ordinary feelings, honor withers; when statutes multiply, people learn only to dodge punishment.

sentences.zh: 孔子曰：「導之以政，齊之以刑，民免而無恥。

translations.literal: Confucius said, "Lead them with administrative measures and regulate them with punishments, and the people will evade but have no sense of shame.
translations.idiomatic: Confucius said, "If you herd the people with edicts and lash them with penalties, they will keep out of trouble but lose all sense of shame.

sentences.zh: 導之以德，齊之以禮，有恥且格。

translations.literal: Lead them with virtue and regulate them with ritual, and they will have a sense of shame and moreover will correct themselves."
translations.idiomatic: Lead them with virtue and restrain them with ritual, and they will feel shame and set themselves right."

sentences.zh: 」古之明王，深識遠慮，動居其厚，不務多辟，周之五刑，不過三千。

translations.literal: "The enlightened kings of antiquity, with deep knowledge and far planning, in action dwelt in the thick and did not strive for many punishments; the five punishments of Zhou did not exceed three thousand.'
translations.idiomatic: The wise kings of old looked far ahead and acted with generous restraint, never multiplying executions: even Zhou's five punishments numbered fewer than three thousand clauses.'

sentences.zh: 大漢初興，詳鑒失得，故破矩為圓，斫雕為樸，蠲除苛政，更立疏網，海內歡欣，人懷寬德。

translations.literal: At the first rise of Great Han, it examined in detail loss and gain; therefore it broke the square to make round, hewed carving to make plain, abolished harsh government, and established anew a loose net; within the seas there was joy, and people cherished generous virtue.
translations.idiomatic: When Han first rose it weighed past mistakes, rounded harsh corners into mercy, stripped ornament down to simplicity, swept away cruel edicts, and cast a wider net—so the realm rejoiced and the people felt the ruler's kindness.

sentences.zh: 及至其後，漸以滋章，吹毛索疵，詆欺無限。

translations.literal: When it reached afterward, gradually it became luxuriant; blowing hair to seek flaws, slander and deceit without limit.
translations.idiomatic: Later ages piled statute upon statute, hunting for fault in every hairbreadth, until slander and spite knew no limit.

sentences.zh: 果桃菜茹之饋，集以成臧，小事無妨於義，以為大戮，故國無廉士，家無完行。

translations.literal: Gifts of fruit, peaches, and vegetables, gathered to constitute theft; small matters without harm to righteousness, taken as great execution—therefore the state had no incorrupt scholars, and families had no complete conduct.
translations.idiomatic: A gift of fruit or greens could be tallied into a theft charge; trivial acts that touched no moral core were punished like capital crimes—so honest men vanished from public life and no household kept an unstained name.

sentences.zh: 至於法不能禁，令不能止，上下相遁。

translations.literal: As for law being unable to prohibit and orders being unable to stop, high and low evade one another.
translations.idiomatic: When law cannot hold what it bans and decrees cannot stop what they forbid, ruler and ruled simply dodge one another.

sentences.zh: 為敝彌深。

translations.literal: The harm done becomes ever deeper.
translations.idiomatic: The harm only deepens.

sentences.zh: 臣愚以為宜如舊制，不合翻移。

translations.literal: Your servant foolishly thinks it is fitting to follow the old system and not fit to overturn and shift.'
translations.idiomatic: I humbly urge that we keep the founding statutes and not overturn them now.'

sentences.zh: 』帝從之。

translations.literal: 'The emperor followed it.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor accepted his advice.

sentences.zh: 後皇太子彊求乞自退，封東海王，故重選官屬，以林為王傅。

translations.literal: Later the crown prince Qiang requested to beg to retire of himself, was enfeoffed as king of Donghai, and therefore officials were anew heavily selected; Lin was made royal tutor to the king.
translations.idiomatic: When Crown Prince Liu Qiang asked to step down and was enfeoffed as king of Donghai, the court refilled his household staff and named Du Lin his tutor.

sentences.zh: 從駕南巡狩。

translations.literal: He followed the imperial carriage on southern tour and hunt.
translations.idiomatic: He accompanied the emperor on a southern inspection tour.

sentences.zh: 時諸王傅數被引命，或多交遊，不得應詔；

translations.literal: At that time the royal tutors were repeatedly summoned and ordered, or many had social engagements and could not answer the edict;
translations.idiomatic: Royal tutors were often summoned on short notice, yet many were off socializing and missed the call;

sentences.zh: 唯林守慎，有召必至。

translations.literal: only Lin kept cautious vigilance; whenever summoned he always arrived.
translations.idiomatic: only Du Lin stayed watchful and answered every summons.

sentences.zh: 餘人雖不見譴，而林特受賞賜，又辭不敢受，帝益重之。

translations.literal: Though the others were not seen to be rebuked, yet Lin alone received rewards and gifts, and moreover declined and did not dare accept them; the emperor increasingly esteemed him.
translations.idiomatic: The rest were not punished, but Du Lin alone received imperial largesse—which he declined—so the emperor prized him all the more.

sentences.zh: 明年，代丁恭為少府。

translations.literal: The next year, he replaced Ding Gong as Privy Treasurer.
translations.idiomatic: The following year he succeeded Ding Gong as privy treasurer.

sentences.zh: 二十二年，復為光祿勛。

translations.literal: In the twenty-second year, he again became Bearer of the Brightness.
translations.idiomatic: In the twenty-second year he was again appointed bearer of the brightness.

sentences.zh: 頃之，代朱浮為大司空。

translations.literal: Before long, he replaced Zhu Fu as Grand Minister of Works.
translations.idiomatic: Soon after he replaced Zhu Fu as grand minister of works.

sentences.zh: 博雅多通，稱為任職相。

translations.literal: He was broad, elegant, and broadly conversant, and was called a minister of works equal to the task.
translations.idiomatic: Learned, polished, and versatile, he was hailed as a minister of works who truly filled the role.

sentences.zh: 明年薨，帝親自臨喪送葬，除子喬為郎。

translations.literal: The next year he died; the emperor personally attended the mourning and sent off the funeral, and appointed his son Qiao Gentleman.
translations.idiomatic: He died the next year; the emperor attended his obsequies in person and appointed his son Du Qiao a gentleman cadet.

sentences.zh: 詔曰：『公侯子孫，必復其始，賢者之後，宜宰城邑。

translations.literal: An edict stated, 'The descendants of dukes and marquises must return to their beginnings; after the worthy, it is fitting to govern cities and towns.
translations.idiomatic: An edict read, 'The sons of nobles must return to their roots, and a sage's heir should rule a county seat.

sentences.zh: 其以喬為丹水長。』

translations.literal: Let Qiao be made chief of Danshui.'
translations.idiomatic: Let Du Qiao be named chief of Danshui.'

sentences.zh: 論曰：夫威強以自禦，力損則身危；

translations.literal: The disquisition says: By might and strength to defend oneself—when strength is exhausted then the body is in peril;
translations.idiomatic: The historian remarks: lean on force alone to guard yourself, and when strength fails you stand in peril;

sentences.zh: 飾詐以圖己，詐窮則道屈；

translations.literal: by adornment and deceit to scheme for oneself—when deceit is exhausted then the Way is bent;
translations.idiomatic: dress up fraud to serve your ends, and when the mask slips your purpose is broken;

sentences.zh: 而忠信篤敬，蠻貊行焉者，誠以德之感物厚矣。

translations.literal: yet loyalty, trustworthiness, earnestness, and respect—even the Man and Mo will practice them—indeed it is because sincerity's moving of things is thick.
translations.idiomatic: yet loyalty, good faith, and reverence move even barbarians, for nothing sways others like genuine virtue.

sentences.zh: 故趙孟懷忠，匹夫成其仁；

translations.literal: Therefore Zhao Meng cherished loyalty, and a common man completed his benevolence;
translations.idiomatic: Thus Zhao Meng clung to good faith and a commoner could accomplish humanity;

sentences.zh: 杜林行義，烈士假其命。

translations.literal: Du Lin practiced righteousness, and a man of fierce resolve borrowed his life.
translations.idiomatic: Du Lin lived by duty, and a desperate man spared his life for it.

sentences.zh: 《易》曰：『人之所助者信』，有不誣矣。

translations.literal: The Changes states, 'What men help is trustworthiness'—there is no falsehood in this.'
translations.idiomatic: The Book of Changes says, 'Heaven aids the trustworthy'—and the text does not lie.'

sentences.zh: 郭丹字少卿，南陽穰人也。

translations.literal: Guo Dan, courtesy name Shaoqing, was a man of Rang in Nanyang commandery.
translations.idiomatic: Guo Dan, styled Shaoqing, was from Rang in Nanyang.

sentences.zh: 父稚，成帝時為廬江太守，有清名。

translations.literal: His father Zhi, in the time of Chengdi, was grand administrator of Lujiang and had a pure reputation.
translations.idiomatic: His father Guo Zhi served as grand administrator of Lujiang under Emperor Cheng and was famed for integrity.

sentences.zh: 丹七歲而孤，小心孝順，後母哀憐之，為鬻衣裝，買產業。

translations.literal: Dan at seven sui was orphaned; he was careful and filial; the stepmother pitied him and for him sold clothing and gear and bought property.
translations.idiomatic: Guo Dan lost his father at seven; he was dutiful and careful, so his stepmother pitied him, sold her ornaments, and bought him land and goods.

sentences.zh: 後從師長安，買符入函谷關，乃慨然嘆曰：『丹不乘使者車，終不出關。

translations.literal: Later he followed a teacher to Chang'an; buying a tally he entered Hangu Pass, and thereupon sighed with feeling, saying, 'Dan, if I do not ride an envoy's carriage, in the end I will not leave the pass.'
translations.idiomatic: Later he studied in Chang'an; when he bought his pass to enter Hangu Gate he swore, 'If I do not ride out as an imperial envoy, I will not cross this barrier again.'

sentences.zh: 』既至京師，常為都講，諸儒鹹敬重之。

translations.literal: When he had reached the capital, he constantly was chief lecturer, and the various Confucians all respected him.
translations.idiomatic: In the capital he regularly served as chief lecturer, and the scholars held him in high regard.

sentences.zh: 大司馬嚴尤請丹，辭病不就。

translations.literal: Grand Marshal Yan You invited Dan; he declined illness and did not take up the post.
translations.idiomatic: Grand Marshal Yan You summoned him, but he pleaded illness and stayed away.

sentences.zh: 王莽又征之，遂與諸生逃於北地。

translations.literal: Wang Mang again summoned him; he thereupon with various students fled to Beidi.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Mang summoned him again, so he fled north into Beidi with a band of students.

sentences.zh: 更始二年，三公舉丹賢能，征為諫議大夫，持節使歸南陽，安集受降。

translations.literal: In the second year of Gengshi, the Three Dukes recommended Dan as worthy and able; he was summoned to be Grandee Remonstrant, bearing credentials was sent to return to Nanyang, to pacify and gather those who surrendered.
translations.idiomatic: In Gengshi 2 the three excellencies nominated him as worthy and able; he was called to be grandee remonstrant, given credentials, and sent south to Nanyang to pacify the people and receive surrenders.

sentences.zh: 丹自去家十有二年，果乘高車出關，如其誌焉。

translations.literal: Dan himself had left home twelve years; in the end he rode a high carriage out of the pass, as his purpose had been.
translations.idiomatic: Twelve years after leaving home he did ride a tall carriage out through the pass, exactly as he had vowed.

sentences.zh: 更始敗，諸將悉歸光武，並獲封爵；

translations.literal: When Gengshi fell, the various generals all returned to Guangwu and together received enfeoffments and noble ranks;
translations.idiomatic: When the Gengshi regime collapsed, every general rushed to Emperor Guangwu and won titles;

sentences.zh: 丹獨保平氏不下，為更始發喪，衰绖盡哀。

translations.literal: Dan alone kept Pingshi from submitting, for Gengshi held mourning, wearing mourning headband and belt to exhaust grief.
translations.idiomatic: Guo Dan alone held Pingshi against surrender, then mourned the Gengshi Emperor in full sackcloth.

sentences.zh: 建武二年，遂潛逃去，敝衣間行，涉歷險阻，求謁更始妻子，奉還節傳，因歸鄉裏。

translations.literal: In the second year of Jianwu, he thereupon secretly fled away, in worn clothing traveled by byways, crossed and experienced peril and obstruction, sought audience with Gengshi's wife and children, presented and returned credentials and tallies, and thereupon returned to his village.
translations.idiomatic: In Jianwu 2 he slipped away in rags by back roads, braved every danger, found the Gengshi emperor's family, returned his official seals, and made his way home.

sentences.zh: 太守杜詩請為功曹，丹薦鄉人長者自代而去。

translations.literal: Grand Administrator Du Shi invited him to be merit assessor; Dan recommended an elder of the village in his place and departed.
translations.idiomatic: Grand Administrator Du Shi offered him the post of merit assessor; Guo Dan named a respected townsman to replace him and left.

sentences.zh: 詩乃嘆曰：『昔明王興化，卿士讓位，今功曹推賢，可謂至德。

translations.literal: Shi thereupon sighed, saying, 'In antiquity when enlightened kings transformed customs, ministers yielded place; now the merit assessor pushes forward the worthy—this can be called utmost virtue.
translations.idiomatic: Du Shi sighed, 'When enlightened kings reformed the realm, high ministers yielded rank; today a merit assessor yields his seat to a better man—that is consummate virtue.

sentences.zh: 敕以丹事編署黃堂，以為後法。』

translations.literal: Order that Dan's affair be compiled and posted on the Yellow Hall, to be a model for later.'
translations.idiomatic: Inscribe Guo Dan's deed on the Yellow Hall as a precedent for the future.'

sentences.zh: 十三年，大司馬吳漢辟舉高第，再遷並州牧，有清平稱。

translations.literal: In the thirteenth year, Grand Marshal Wu Han recommended him in the highest grade; he was twice transferred to Governor of Bing province and had a reputation for purity and fairness.
translations.idiomatic: In the thirteenth year Grand Marshal Wu Han nominated him top of the list; after two promotions he became governor of Bingzhou, famed for an even and incorrupt administration.

sentences.zh: 轉使匈奴中郎將，遷左馮翊。

translations.literal: He was transferred to General of the Household Gentlemen in Charge of the Xiongnu, then transferred to left Administrator of Fengyi.
translations.idiomatic: He moved on to serve as general of the household in charge of the Xiongnu envoys, then became left administrator of Fengyi.

sentences.zh: 永平三年，代李訢為司徒。

translations.literal: In the third year of Yongping, he replaced Li Xin as Minister of Education.
translations.idiomatic: In Yongping 3 he succeeded Li Xin as minister of education.

sentences.zh: 在朝廉直公正，與侯霸、杜林、張湛、郭伋齊名相善。

translations.literal: At court he was incorrupt, straight, and fair, and with Hou Ba, Du Lin, Zhang Zhan, and Guo Ji he shared a name and was on good terms.
translations.idiomatic: At court he was known for integrity and even-handed justice, and stood on the same footing as Hou Ba, Du Lin, Zhang Zhan, and Guo Ji.

sentences.zh: 明年，坐考隴西太守鄧融事無所據，策免。

translations.literal: The next year, for sitting in judgment on the affair of grand administrator Deng Rong of Longxi without basis, he was dismissed by rescript.
translations.idiomatic: The next year he lost his post for pressing a case against Deng Rong, grand administrator of Longxi, without evidence.

sentences.zh: 五年，卒於家，時年八十七。

translations.literal: In the fifth year, he died at home, aged eighty-seven.
translations.idiomatic: He died at home in the fifth year, at the age of eighty-seven.

sentences.zh: 以河南尹範遷有清行，代為司徒。

translations.literal: Because Fan Qian, metropolitan governor of Henan, had pure conduct, he replaced him as Minister of Education.
translations.idiomatic: Fan Qian of Henan, noted for clean living, was named minister of education in his place.

sentences.zh: 遷字子廬，沛國人，初為漁陽太守，以智略安邊，匈奴不敢入界。

translations.literal: Qian, courtesy name Zilu, was a man of the Principality of Pei; at first he was grand administrator of Yuyang, and by strategy and planning he pacified the border, so the Xiongnu did not dare enter his territory.
translations.idiomatic: Fan Qian, styled Zilu, came from the princedom of Pei. As grand administrator of Yuyang he secured the frontier by shrewd policy, and the Xiongnu would not cross his border.

sentences.zh: 及在公輔，有宅數畝，田不過一頃，復推與兄子。

translations.literal: When among the highest ministers, he had a residence of several mu and fields not exceeding one qing, yet again he pushed them to his elder brother's son.
translations.idiomatic: Even as one of the chief ministers he owned only a few mu of house and less than one qing of land, which he then signed over to his nephew.

sentences.zh: 其妻嘗謂曰：『君有四子而無立錐之地，可余奉祿，以為後世業。

translations.literal: His wife once said to him, 'You have four sons yet not land to stick an awl; you could set aside your salary to build an estate for posterity.'
translations.idiomatic: His wife once said, 'You have four sons and not a handspan of soil—why not salt away your salary for their future.'

sentences.zh: 』遷曰：『吾備位大臣而蓄財求利，何以示後世！

translations.literal: Qian said, 'I occupy a minister's place yet hoard wealth and seek profit—how would I show posterity!'
translations.idiomatic: Fan Qian replied, 'I sit among the great ministers; if I piled up silver for my heirs, what example would I leave!'

sentences.zh: 』在位四年薨，家無擔石焉。

translations.literal: After four years in office he died; the family had not a dan or shi of grain.
translations.idiomatic: He died in office after four years, leaving his household without a peck to spare.

sentences.zh: 後顯宗因朝會問群臣：『郭丹家今何如？

translations.literal: Later, at court assembly, Emperor Xianzong asked the host of ministers, 'How is Guo Dan's family now?'
translations.idiomatic: Later Emperor Ming asked his ministers at audience, 'What has become of Guo Dan's family?'

sentences.zh: 』宗正劉匡對曰：『昔孫叔敖相楚，馬不秣粟，妻不衣帛，子孫竟蒙寢丘之封。

translations.literal: Director of the Imperial Clan Liu Kuang replied, 'In the past Sunshu Ao was minister of Chu: his horses were not fed grain, his wife did not wear silk, yet in the end his descendants received the fief of Qiuqiu.'
translations.idiomatic: Liu Kuang, director of the imperial clan, answered, 'Sunshu Ao served Chu with such austerity that his horses ate no grain and his wife wore no silk, yet his line still won the fief of Qiuqiu.'

sentences.zh: 丹出典州郡，入為三公，而家無遺產，子孫困匱。

translations.literal: Dan went out to govern provinces and commanderies and entered as one of the Three Dukes, yet his house had no leftover property, and sons and grandsons were in want.
translations.idiomatic: Guo Dan rose from provincial posts to the three excellencies, yet died without an estate; his descendants were left destitute.

sentences.zh: 』帝乃下南陽訪求其嗣。

translations.literal: The emperor thereupon sent orders down to Nanyang to search out his heirs.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor then commanded Nanyang to seek out his posterity.

sentences.zh: 長子宇，官至常山太守。

translations.literal: The eldest son Yu, office reached grand administrator of Changshan.
translations.idiomatic: His eldest son Guo Yu rose to be grand administrator of Changshan.

sentences.zh: 少子濟，趙相。

translations.literal: The younger son Ji, minister of Zhao.
translations.idiomatic: His younger son Guo Ji became minister of Zhao.

sentences.zh: 吳良字大儀，齊國臨淄人也。

translations.literal: Wu Liang, courtesy name Dayi, was a man of Linzi in the Principality of Qi.
translations.idiomatic: Wu Liang, styled Dayi, was a native of Linzi in Qi.

sentences.zh: 初為郡吏，歲旦與掾史入賀，門下掾王望舉觴上壽，謅稱太守功德。

translations.literal: At first he was a clerk of the commandery; on New Year's dawn he entered with the clerks and assistants to congratulate; the gatehouse clerk Wang Wang raised a cup to toast long life, flatteringly praising the grand administrator's merit and virtue.
translations.idiomatic: He began as a commandery clerk. On New Year's morning he went in with the staff to pay respects; the porter Wang Wang raised a cup, gushing praise of the grand administrator's achievements.

sentences.zh: 良於下坐勃然進曰：『望佞邪之人，欺謅無狀，願勿受其觴。

translations.literal: Liang from the lower seat suddenly advanced and said, 'Wang is a fawning and evil man who deceives and flatters without restraint; I beg you not to accept his cup.'
translations.idiomatic: From the back row Wu Liang sprang up and said, 'Wang is a sycophant whose praise is empty flattery—do not drink his toast.'

sentences.zh: 』太守斂容而止。

translations.literal: The grand administrator composed his countenance and stopped.
translations.idiomatic: The grand administrator checked himself and set the cup down.

sentences.zh: 宴罷，轉良為功曹；

translations.literal: When the banquet ended, he transferred Liang to merit assessor;
translations.idiomatic: When the feast broke up, the governor named Wu Liang merit assessor;

sentences.zh: 恥以言受進，終不肯謁。

translations.literal: ashamed to advance by words, to the end he was unwilling to pay formal visit.
translations.idiomatic: but Wu Liang was ashamed to owe the post to flattery and never reported for duty.

sentences.zh: 時，驃騎將軍東平王蒼聞而辟之，署為西曹。

translations.literal: At that time General of Agile Cavalry, Prince Cang of Dongping, heard of it and summoned him, appointing him to the western bureau.
translations.idiomatic: General of agile cavalry, Prince Liu Cang of Dongping, heard of him and took him on as a western-bureau clerk.

sentences.zh: 蒼甚相敬受，上疏薦良曰：『臣聞為國所重，必在得人；

translations.literal: Cang greatly respected and received him, and submitted a memorial recommending Liang, saying, 'Your servant has heard that what the state esteems must lie in obtaining the right men;
translations.idiomatic: Prince Cang esteemed him deeply and memorialized: 'What a state must prize above all is the right men in office;

sentences.zh: 報恩之義，莫大薦士。

translations.literal: in the meaning of repaying favor, nothing is greater than recommending scholars.'
translations.idiomatic: and nothing repays a sovereign's kindness like lifting worthy men to his notice.'

sentences.zh: 竊見臣府西曹掾齊國吳良，資質敦固，公方廉恪，躬儉安貧，白首一節；

translations.literal: Your servant has privately observed the western-bureau clerk Wu Liang of Qi in my household: his endowment is honest and firm, he is upright, incorrupt, and reverent, personally frugal and content in poverty, white-haired yet of one integrity;
translations.idiomatic: Your servant's western-bureau clerk Wu Liang of Qi is steadfast by nature, square and scrupulous, content in poverty though gray-haired, unwavering in principle;

sentences.zh: 又治《尚書》，學通師法，經任博士，行中表儀。

translations.literal: moreover he has studied the Documents, his learning penetrates the master's methods, he could fill the post of erudite, and in conduct he is a model inside and out.
translations.idiomatic: he has mastered the Book of Documents in the orthodox school tradition, is fit to be an erudite, and his deportment is a mirror for others.

sentences.zh: 宜備宿衛，以輔聖政。

translations.literal: It is fitting to assign him to night guard to assist sagely government.'
translations.idiomatic: He should join the palace guard to help perfect your government.'

sentences.zh: 臣蒼榮寵絕矣，憂責深大，私慕公叔同升之義，懼於臧文竊位之罪，敢秉愚瞽，犯冒嚴禁。

translations.literal: Your servant Cang's glory and favor are already extreme; my burden of responsibility is deep and great; privately I admire Uncle Gong's meaning of rising together, and fear the crime of Zang Wenrong's stealing a place; I dare, holding my foolish blindness, to offend against stern prohibitions.'
translations.idiomatic: I have received more honor than I deserve and a heavier charge than I can bear; I admire the minister who promoted his better alongside himself and dread the reproach of the man who hid talent—so I risk your displeasure to speak.'

sentences.zh: 』顯宗以示公卿曰：『前以事見良，須發皓然，衣冠甚偉。

translations.literal: Xianzong showed it to the dukes and ministers, saying, 'Earlier I saw Liang on business: his beard and hair were hoary white, his cap and robes very imposing.'
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Ming showed the memorial to the high ministers, saying, 'I met Wu Liang on business once: his hair was snow-white, his dress magnificent.'

sentences.zh: 夫薦賢助國，宰相之職，蕭何舉韓信，設壇而拜，不復考試。

translations.literal: To recommend the worthy and assist the state is the duty of the minister of education; Xiao He recommended Han Xin, set up the altar and invested him, without again examining and testing.'
translations.idiomatic: Recommending talent is the chancellor's work: Xiao He raised Han Xin to general on a single ceremony, with no further examination.'

sentences.zh: 今以良為議郎。』

translations.literal: Now appoint Liang Gentleman Consultant.'
translations.idiomatic: Let Wu Liang be appointed gentleman consultant.'

sentences.zh: 永平中，車駕近出，而信陽侯陰就幹突禁衛，車府令徐匡鉤就車，收禦者送獄。

translations.literal: In the Yongping period, the imperial carriage went out on a near excursion, and Marquis of Xinyang Yin Jiu burst through the forbidden guard; the carriage-house director Xu Kuang hooked Jiu's carriage and arrested the driver and sent him to prison.
translations.idiomatic: During Yongping the emperor rode out on a short tour; Marquis Yin Jiu of Xinyang forced his way through the palace guard; carriage-house director Xu Kuang seized his chariot and jailed the charioteer.

sentences.zh: 詔書譴匡，匡乃自系。

translations.literal: An edict rebuked Kuang; Kuang thereupon bound himself.
translations.idiomatic: An edict rebuked Xu Kuang, who then placed himself in fetters.

sentences.zh: 良上言曰：『信陽侯就倚恃外戚，幹犯乘輿，無人臣禮，為大不敬。

translations.literal: Liang submitted words, saying, 'Marquis of Xinyang Jiu relies on being maternal kin, violates the imperial equipage, lacks the ritual of minister and subject, and is greatly irreverent.'
translations.idiomatic: Wu Liang memorialized: 'Yin Jiu trades on his status as an imperial in-law, obstructs the imperial train, and shows none of a subject's deference—that is grave disrespect.'

sentences.zh: 匡執法守正，反下於理，臣恐聖化由是而弛。

translations.literal: Kuang in holding the law and guarding rectitude is instead sent down to the judicial authorities; your servant fears sagely transformation will thereby slacken.'
translations.idiomatic: Yet Xu Kuang, who only upheld the law, faces the judge himself—I fear your moral sway will slacken if this stands.'

sentences.zh: 』帝雖赦匡，猶左轉良為即丘長。

translations.literal: Although the emperor pardoned Kuang, he still demoted Liang leftward to be chief of Jiuqiu.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor pardoned Xu Kuang but still demoted Wu Liang to magistrate of Jiuqiu.

sentences.zh: 後遷司徒長史。

translations.literal: Later he was transferred to senior clerk of the Minister of Education.
translations.idiomatic: He was later promoted to senior clerk under the minister of education.

sentences.zh: 每處大議，輒據經典，不希旨偶俗，以僥時譽。

translations.literal: Whenever seated in great deliberation, he always relied on the classics, did not hope for imperial intent or echo vulgar opinion, in order to seek timely praise.
translations.idiomatic: In every great debate he cited the classics, never trimming his views to court fashion or chasing cheap applause.

sentences.zh: 後坐事免，復拜議郎，卒於官。

translations.literal: Later for sitting in an affair he was dismissed; again he was appointed Gentleman Consultant and died in office.
translations.idiomatic: He was later removed over a case, then recalled as gentleman consultant and died in that post.

sentences.zh: 承宮字少子，瑯邪姑幕人也。

translations.literal: Cheng Gong, courtesy name Shaozi, was a man of Gumu in Langya commandery.
translations.idiomatic: Cheng Gong, styled Shaozi, came from Gumu in Langya.

sentences.zh: 少孤，年八歲為人牧豕。

translations.literal: In youth he was orphaned; at eight sui he herded pigs for people.
translations.idiomatic: Orphaned young, at eight he hired out as a swineherd.

sentences.zh: 鄉裏徐子盛者，以《春秋經》授諸生數百人，宮過息廬下，樂其業，因就聽經，遂請留門下，為諸生拾薪。

translations.literal: In the village there was Xu Zisheng, who with the Spring and Autumn classic instructed several hundred students; Gong passed and rested beneath his hut, delighted with their undertaking, thereupon drew near to listen to the classic, then requested to remain at the gate, gathering firewood for the students.
translations.idiomatic: A neighbor, Xu Zisheng, taught the Spring and Autumn Annals to hundreds of pupils. Cheng Gong rested under his eaves, loved what he heard, asked to stay on as fuel-gatherer for the school.

sentences.zh: 執苦數年，勤學不倦。

translations.literal: He endured hardship several years, studied diligently without weariness.
translations.idiomatic: For years he labored without flagging at his books.

sentences.zh: 經典既明，乃歸家教授。

translations.literal: When the classics were already clear, he returned home and taught.
translations.idiomatic: Once he had mastered the texts he went home to teach on his own.

sentences.zh: 遭天下喪亂，遂將諸生避地漢中，後與妻子之蒙陰山，肆力耕種。

translations.literal: Encountering the world's bereavement and disorder, he thereupon led the various students to flee and settle in Hanzhong; later with wife and children he went to Mengyin Mountain and exerted strength in plowing and planting.
translations.idiomatic: When chaos swept the realm he led his pupils to refuge in Hanzhong, then moved with his family to Mengyin Mountain and farmed with all his might.

sentences.zh: 禾黍將孰，人有認之者，宮不與計，推之而去，由是顯名。

translations.literal: When the grain was about to ripen, someone claimed it was his; Gong did not dispute with him, pushed it aside and departed, whereby he became famous.
translations.idiomatic: As the crop ripened a neighbor claimed the field; Cheng Gong yielded the grain without argument and walked away, and his name spread.

sentences.zh: 三府更辟，皆不應。

translations.literal: The three offices repeatedly summoned him; all he did not answer.
translations.idiomatic: The three highest bureaus summoned him in turn; he refused every call.

sentences.zh: 鄭均字仲虞，東平任城人也。

translations.literal: Zheng Jun, courtesy name Zhongyu, was a man of Rencheng in Dongping commandery.
translations.idiomatic: Zheng Jun, styled Zhongyu, was from Rencheng in Dongping.

sentences.zh: 少好黃、老書。

translations.literal: In youth he was fond of the books of the Yellow Emperor and Laozi.
translations.idiomatic: In youth he studied the Yellow Emperor and Laozi.

sentences.zh: 兄為縣吏，頗受禮遺，均數諫止，不聽。

translations.literal: His elder brother was a county clerk and rather received gifts and presents; Jun several times remonstrated to stop him, but he did not listen.
translations.idiomatic: His elder brother served as a county clerk and took bribes; Zheng Jun warned him again and again, without effect.

sentences.zh: 即脫身為傭，歲餘，得錢帛，歸以與兄。

translations.literal: Thereupon he left his person to become a hired laborer; after more than a year he obtained cash and silk, returned and gave them to his elder brother.
translations.idiomatic: He hired himself out as a laborer; after a year he brought home coin and silk for his brother.

sentences.zh: 曰：『物盡可復得，為吏坐臧，終身捐棄。

translations.literal: He said, 'Things when exhausted can be obtained again; to be a clerk and sit for corruption goods is to be cast off for life.'
translations.idiomatic: He said, 'Goods can always be replaced, but a clerk convicted of graft is ruined forever.'

sentences.zh: 』兄感其言，遂為廉潔。

translations.literal: The elder brother was moved by his words and thereupon became incorrupt and clean.
translations.idiomatic: The brother took the lesson to heart and mended his ways.

sentences.zh: 均好義篤實，養寡嫂孤兒，恩禮敦至。

translations.literal: Jun was fond of righteousness, earnest and solid; he raised his widowed sister-in-law and orphan children, favor and ritual were thick and thorough.
translations.idiomatic: Zheng Jun loved duty and meant what he said; he supported his widowed sister-in-law and her orphans with unstinting kindness.

sentences.zh: 常稱病家廷，不應州郡辟召。

translations.literal: He constantly claimed illness at home in the courtyard, and did not answer province and commandery summons.
translations.idiomatic: He habitually pleaded illness at home and ignored every provincial summons.

sentences.zh: 郡將欲必致之，使縣令譎將詣門，既至，卒不能屈。

translations.literal: The commandery general wished necessarily to get him and ordered the county magistrate deceitfully to escort him to the gate; when he had arrived, in the end could not bend him.
